Headline

Uniswap Officially Launches Robinhood Chain

On July 2nd, Uniswap announced on its X platform that Robinhood Chain, a Layer 2 network built by the Robinhood crypto business team, has been fully launched on Uniswap V2, V3, V4, and the UniswapX protocol. Uniswap is the core public Automated Market Maker (AMM) on Robinhood Chain, and on its first day of launch, it simultaneously supports the Uniswap web interface, wallet client, and API. The uniswap-trading-tools AI plugin, integrating three new functions, is also coming soon. Now, users can use Uniswap to complete token exchanges, provide liquidity, purchase stock tokens, deploy AI trading agents on Robinhood Chain, and experience this Layer 2 network in depth.

... This mature product system, trusted by millions of users, is now available on RobinhoodChain.

      AI Hot Topics

      1. Federal Reserve's Daly: We are in the early stages of AI potentially leading to exponential productivity growth

      On July 2nd, Federal Reserve's Daly stated that the impact of artificial intelligence investment has raised concerns about whether it will cause inflation. If central banks act too quickly, they may prematurely suppress the economy; if they act too slowly, it may have adverse effects on the public. We are in the early stages of artificial intelligence potentially leading to exponential productivity growth.

      2. Kuaishou's Keling AI to Complete $3 Billion Funding Round, Valuation at $18 Billion, Tencent Participates

      On July 2nd, according to sources, Kuaishou's video generation business, Keling AI, will complete a $3 billion (approximately RMB 20.38 billion) funding round, bringing its post-investment valuation to $18 billion (approximately RMB 122.3 billion), a $2 billion decrease from the initial $20 billion valuation target set in April this year. Tencent participated in this round of financing. A person close to the deal revealed that Kuaishou expects to initiate the Hong Kong listing process for Keling AI within the next 12 months. The IPO proceeds will primarily be used to expand computing power, build data centers, and attract and retain talent. Kuaishou, Keling AI, and Tencent did not immediately respond to requests for comment.

      ...

      3. JPMorgan Chase: AI Chip Stock Rally May Be Unsustainable

      JPMorgan Chase stated that AI chip stocks may struggle to outperform hyperscale cloud service providers in the long run. JPMorgan Chase predicts that either increased monetization of AI will help cloud service providers catch up, or massive spending in the AI ​​sector may ultimately curb capital expenditures by hyperscale cloud service providers, thus weakening chip demand. While JPMorgan Chase leans towards an optimistic outlook, a slowdown in expected capital expenditures remains a major risk for semiconductor stocks.

      4. DeepSeek Service Restored After About an Hour Outage

      July 2nd news: Around 16:40 this afternoon, the DeepSeek service status page displayed abnormal webpage/API performance. Around 17:50, the status page indicated that the problem had been resolved and service had been restored.

      5. Financial Times: OpenAI Proposes 5% Stake in Trump Administration On July 2nd, the Financial Times reported that OpenAI proposed offering a 5% stake in the Trump administration. DeFi Hot Topics 1. Ondo Finance Launches Ethereum-Based Custodial Tokenized Securities Solution in the US Ondo Finance announced the launch of a third-party custodial tokenized securities solution operating within the existing regulatory framework in the United States. It has partnered with Broadridge Financial Solutions to provide tokenized stock holders with full shareholder governance and voting rights support. According to the mechanism design, the underlying stock remains within the traditional regulated custody system in the United States. Tokens are minted at a 1:1 ratio by Ondo's registered transfer agent and issued on the Ethereum blockchain, while assets are held by a compliant custodian.

      2. Taiko Announces Bridge Service Returned to Normal, Network Fully Resumed Operation

      On July 2nd, according to official news, the previously affected cross-chain bridging function has been repaired, and bidirectional asset transfers between the Taiko network and external networks have now reopened. The team stated that "Step 4 of the repair is complete," the network has fully returned to normal operation, user asset status has returned to consistency, and any existing restrictions will not affect normal use. At the same time, the official team reminds users to be aware of security risks: they will not proactively send private messages to users, and there are no airdrop or claim websites; only information from official accounts is reliable. The team stated that a full incident review report will be released later.

      3. Kalshi and Polymarket's combined trading volume reached $44.8 billion in June, a 75% increase month-over-month.

      On July 2nd, according to The Block, driven by the World Cup, Kalshi and Polymarket's combined trading volume reached $44.8 billion in June, a 75% increase month-over-month, compared to $25.66 billion in the previous month. Specifically, Kalshi's volume increased from $16.81 billion to $31.5 billion, a month-over-month increase of 87.4%; Polymarket's main platform (non-US) increased from $7.08 billion to $10.26 billion, a month-over-month increase of 45%; and Polymarket US increased from $1.77 billion to $3.04 billion. Kalshi's World Cup winner prediction market attracted over $832 million in bets, with approximately 35% betting on France to win; Polymarket's World Cup match contracts attracted between $500,000 and $2 million in trading volume per match.

      ...

      4. dYdX Launches Arcus, a DEX Based on Robinhood Chain

      On July 2nd, dYdX Labs announced the launch of Arcus, a decentralized exchange based on Robinhood Chain, supporting 24/7 trading of 95 stock tokens, staking perpetual contracts, and mainstream cryptocurrencies, with zero fees for spot trading of stock tokens. Market data shows that the price of DYDX quickly fell back yesterday afternoon, and accelerated its decline after the announcement, currently trading at $0.145, a 24-hour drop of 23.11%. The previous day, influenced by the announcement of a "major announcement," DYDX surged over 40% in 24 hours. The launch of Arcus has raised concerns in the community about whether the new product will affect the independence and governance structure of the existing dYdX Chain. The dYdX Foundation issued a statement explaining that Arcus will not affect the operation of the dYdX Chain, which remains governed by DYDX token holders and secured by a validator network.

      The foundation stated that trading, deposits, withdrawals, staking, governance, and validator operations on the dYdX Chain remain normal; the DYDX token, as the governance and staking token of the dYdX Chain, has no changes in its mechanism, supply, or operation. The foundation also stated that any adjustments to the validator set and related requirements still require approval from the dYdX community governance.

      5. Solana Launches On-Chain Governance Mechanism; Proposals Require 15% Staking Support Before Voting

      On July 2nd, the Solana Foundation announced the official launch of the on-chain governance mechanism, Solana Governance Proposals (SGP). Validators can now submit, support, and decide core protocol decisions through SGP. All proposals are conducted on-chain, using a staking-weighted voting mechanism and verified through Merkle proofs. Any validator with at least 100,000 SOL delegates can initiate an SGP.

      ... Unlike Solana Improvement Documents (SIMD), which focus on technical and protocol changes, SGP is primarily used to express opinions on ecosystem governance. Proposals must receive at least 15% of the network's staking support before entering the formal voting stage. Disclaimer: Jinse Finance, as a blockchain information platform, publishes articles for informational purposes only and does not constitute actual investment advice. Please establish sound investment principles and be sure to enhance your risk awareness.

data.title: Binance's exit: Who's grabbing billions in liquidity in Europe?
data.description:
At dawn on July 1st, the EU's MiCA transition period officially ended.

Of the more than 3,000 platforms, only fewer than 250 ultimately received a "get-out-of-jail-free card,"a pass rate of less than 10%.

Binance is not on the list.

On the same day, licensed exchanges scrambled to snatch up the European users "given away" by Binance: Coinbase offered a 5% transfer reward, OKX increased it to 8%, and Gate and Crypto.com offered deposit rewards of up to 10%. The competitors weren't targeting new users, but rather the existing funds left behind by Binance. The subsidy war started faster than any press release. This wasn't just another typical "exchange withdrawing from a region" narrative; it was a cleanup operation—even the world's largest exchange failed to meet the requirements. This is how the EU, with a single regulation, shut out what CZ called "the world's best liquidity."

 The ebb tide was actually foreshadowed 

MiCA's defeat in this battle is not a crippling blow to Binance—but it has brought to the forefront the bleeding that Binance had already begun last year.

MiCA's defeat in this battle is not a crippling blow to Binance—but it has brought to the forefront the bleeding that Binance had already begun last year.

MiCA's defeat in this battle is not a crippling blow to Binance—but it has brought to the forefront the bleeding that Binance had already begun last year.

According to Coingecko's annual report, Binance's total trading volume in 2025 reached $7.3 trillion, with a market share of 39.2%, still appearing to be the leader, but its year-on-year growth rate had already turned negative. By December, although its market share was still 38.3%, its trading volume had plummeted by more than 40% month-on-month. Entering 2026, this trend became even steeper: in February, its market share once fell to 22%, the lowest point since 2020. Although it rebounded somewhat in April, with its derivatives share returning to around 36%, the seeds of the decline had already been sown before the tightening of regulations. OKX Europe CEO Erald Ghoos's assessment was even more direct: approximately 80% of European crypto companies will not survive MiCA. By the last minute, approximately 60% of European users remained on unlicensed platforms—these users weren't unwilling to leave, but simply had nowhere else to go. Not everyone is rushing towards compliance. The Dubai law firm NeosLegal revealed that in the past year and a half, inquiries from European founders have surged, now exceeding 120 per week, half of which originate in Europe. While Europe extends approval cycles to months, Dubai offers an alternative: "licenses issued in days, covering a market of billions." Tighter regulations never eliminate demand; they only push it towards the path of least resistance. The Three Lines of the Clearance: Entry rules, stablecoin platforms, and the actual actions of funds—these three things seem independent, but they are all projections of the same clearing operation on different dimensions. Breaking them down makes them clearer. The first line is the entry rules themselves. MiCA's passport mechanism compresses the entry ticket from 27 countries into a single license: obtaining authorization in any one country theoretically grants access to the entire EU single market. Germany leads with 56 licenses, followed closely by the Netherlands and France. These licenses are highly concentrated in a few jurisdictions willing to attract investment and allocate resources for approvals. This means that the regulatory standards of a small or medium-sized member state effectively determine the entry threshold for the entire EU market—which is why small countries like Greece and Austria have suddenly become so influential. The second front is the stablecoin arena, where the competition has already begun. Circle's USDC received MiCA electronic currency token authorization, becoming the only compliant one among the top ten stablecoins; Tether, unwilling to deposit 60% of its reserves into European bank accounts, directly withdrew its application, leaving the $184 billion USDT excluded from compliant exchanges. Coinbase, Kraken, and Crypto.com successively delisted USDT, and European traders, accustomed to the "default bridge to the US dollar" for years, suddenly found themselves with a different issuer. The third line of action is the actual movement of funds—the day Binance withdrew its application, money had already begun to leave. On the day Binance announced its withdrawal of the Greek MiCA application, net outflows reached $1.96 billion. In the following two days, further outflows of $2.52 billion and $1.46 billion occurred, respectively, bringing the total net outflow to over $400 million within a week. This money didn't wait until the July 1st deadline to move—it started flowing out the very day the news of the withdrawal was announced. Rules, platforms, and funds—these three factors occurred almost simultaneously. Who really profited from this purge? On the surface, it seems like a story of "giants without licenses losing to regulators." But a closer look reveals that those truly reaping the benefits of existing liquidity aren't necessarily the jurisdictions with the most licenses, like Germany and the Netherlands, but rather the players who had already established a "multi-pronged approach"—institutions that had obtained full VASP licenses in Dubai and also had a presence in Luxembourg or Ireland. These were the ones with the highest bargaining power in this purge. They didn't need to choose between Europe and the Middle East; instead, they secured both, waiting to absorb the institutional clients vacated by those being eliminated. The entry of traditional financial institutions is also noteworthy. Standard Chartered wasn't the first bank to apply for a European crypto license, but its decision to announce it the day before the deadline is itself a signal: when compliance costs become so high that only large funds can afford to play, the bargaining power at the table will naturally shift towards traditional financial institutions. This also confirms industry predictions—a wave of mergers and acquisitions is coming, and buyers aren't looking for technology, but rather the license shells held by companies that missed the approval process, rather than those whose businesses are inherently non-compliant. Poland's situation is another overlooked factor: the fact that a country can remain within the EU without establishing a licensing system itself indicates that the "unity" of MiCA still has cracks. Approximately 2,000 unlicensed crypto entities remain in Poland. Where will these existing funds and users go next? This will be the next question to be discussed. The position of retail investors in this game is clear: those users who haven't had time to migrate aren't unwilling to leave, but once their identity verification and transaction records are moved, even if the platform offers lower fees and a better experience in the future, they may not be able to return. The subsidy war is never about grabbing new users, but about this existing capital that is forced to move but has almost nowhere to go. The clearing-out won't only happen in Europe. Applying this logic back to the survival logic of the crypto industry itself, we can see three underlying trends unfolding simultaneously. First, compliant infrastructure is becoming a new scarce resource. Whoever obtains the license first, and whoever solidifies custody and settlement capabilities first, will be able to absorb the institutional funds vacated by those being cleared out—this path applies not only to centralized exchanges, but also to stablecoin issuers and platforms that put RWA assets on-chain. Second, the space for regulatory arbitrage hasn't disappeared; it's just changed its map. When Europe raises the bar, funds and teams will naturally flow to jurisdictions like Dubai, which can issue licenses in days and cover a market of billions. For compliance-sensitive funds, this tightening will create a new window of opportunity for flexible teams. Third, non-compliance demands won't disappear; they will simply shift their focus. Historically, high-frequency trading, high-risk appetite, and non-compliance-sensitive funds have consistently flowed more towards DEXs, non-custodial wallets, and offshore platforms after each tightening of regulations. The MiCA question from the EU isn't a multiple-choice question; it's an elimination round. A license doesn't determine who survives, but without a license, you don't even have the right to sit at the table. The European Commission launched a review consultation on MiCA itself in May, and stablecoin competitiveness, DeFi, staking and lending, and RWA are all within the scope of the renewed discussion—this purge is far from over on July 1st; it has simply moved to a more covert battlefield.

data.title: Has the top spot in Olana's revenue charts changed hands?
data.description:
Author: Chloe, ChainCatcher

Solana's on-chain narrative is expanding from memes to tokenized transaction cards. In the past, Pump.fun was almost the representative application for Solana's consumer revenue; however, after entering the second quarter of 2026, Pump.fun's quarterly revenue began to slow, while Collector Crypt delivered a stronger recent growth curve.

DefiLlama data shows that Pump.fun's revenue in the first quarter was $108.3 million, which dropped to $69.2 million so far in the second quarter, a decrease of 36.1% from the previous quarter. During the same period, Collector Crypt's revenue curve clearly trended upwards ...em>

Collector Crypt's revenue was $12.3 million in the first quarter and has risen to $25.8 million so far in the second quarter, a growth of 108.8%. Its revenue in the most recent week reached $5.1 million, approximately 38% of its total 30-day revenue of nearly $13.5 million. This data suggests that Pump.fun still has a larger scale, but Collector Crypt is gaining stronger short-term growth momentum. The data suggests that perhaps Pump.fun has not lost its scale of growth, but rather that the sources of Solana transaction fees are becoming more diversified. The meme coin market remains vast, but tokenized trading cards, random card packs, physical card redemption, and on-chain secondary markets are emerging as another consumption scenario that generates real revenue, transactions, and user retention. Is Collector Crypt's profit source stable? Collector Crypt's core operating model involves hosting physical, graded trading cards and minting corresponding NFTs on Solana. After purchasing a random card pack, users receive a digital card linked to a physical card; they can then choose to hold the NFT, sell it on-chain, sell it back to the platform, or redeem the physical card. Collector Crypt's revenue primarily comes from random card pack sales, secondary market transaction fees, and royalties; when calculating revenue, the buyback cost incurred by users selling cards back to the platform is also deducted. Furthermore, Collector Crypt purchases trading cards in bulk and acquires inventory at a discount of approximately 5% to 15%. Users who do not wish to keep the cards after opening a pack can sell them back to the platform at a price approximately 7% to 15% lower than the market price. This allows Collector Crypt to retain an operating profit margin of approximately 4% to 5%. According to estimates, for a $1,000 card pack, Collector Crypt's overall profit margin is approximately 5.14%; after deducting incentive costs such as user rewards, the net profit margin is approximately 4.44%. This model has attracted attention because it doesn't just sell on-chain images, but connects on-chain transactions with real-world collectibles. Collector Crypt surpassed $1 billion in cumulative transaction volume in May 2026; the platform opened over 215,000 tokenized trading card packs in a single week, and over 30% of users redeemed physical cards. However, Collector Crypt has not yet truly replaced Pump.fun in terms of total revenue. As of now in 2026, Pump.fun's revenue is approximately $177.5 million, and the Pump ecosystem's total revenue is approximately $466.5 million; in comparison, Collector Crypt's revenue in 2026 was approximately $38.1 million. In terms of cumulative revenue, Pump.fun exceeds $1 billion, the Pump ecosystem's total revenue is approximately $1.18 billion, while Collector Crypt's is approximately $58.4 million. Therefore, the so-called "change of the revenue throne" is more accurately described as a narrative shift in recent revenue momentum and daily revenue rankings, rather than a complete reversal of historical cumulative scale. Pump.fun's model relies on a speculative token issuance cycle: users continuously issue new tokens, trade them on the early price curve, and then some tokens enter the public market, generating fees at each stage. Collector Crypt's model is a different consumption cycle: users buy random card packs, obtain on-chain credentials for corresponding physical collectibles, and then conduct on-chain transactions, instant resale, or physical redemption. Both generate fees, trading volume, and market attention, but the reasons for attracting users are different. Pump.fun leans more towards attention, volatility, and early-stage meme tokens; Collector Crypt leans more towards collectability, scarcity, gamified experience, and real-world asset anchoring. This is also where the focus of discussion regarding Solana consumer apps has shifted: revenue no longer comes solely from meme token issuance but can also come from collectible consumption that is closer to real-world assets. In conclusion, the sustainability of Collector Crypt's revenue depends primarily on whether demand for random card packs can be maintained. If card pack demand remains strong, Collector Crypt's 30-day revenue may continue to approach that of Pump.fun; if demand for random card packs cools down, the recent high concentration of Collector Crypt's revenue could turn from a growth signal into a risk. Additionally, a second growth variable for Collector Crypt is whether it can expand from Pokémon cards to sports cards and other collectibles. Data indicates that Pokémon cards currently dominate Collector Crypt's monthly transaction volume; however, at physical card shows, sports cards and Pokémon cards each account for roughly half of sales, while on-chain sports cards currently only account for 3% to 4% of Collector Crypt's $88 million monthly transaction volume. The third variable for Collector Crypt is regulatory pressure. If multiple regulatory jurisdictions begin to scrutinize random card pack mechanisms using a "blind box regulatory framework," it could slow Collector Crypt's growth. Therefore, assuming the macro environment remains unchanged, Pump.fun will remain the larger revenue engine, and Collector Crypt will continue to appear at the top of the Solana application revenue rankings. In this scenario, Solana's consumer application revenue will no longer rely solely on memecoin issuance but will gradually expand to more consumption scenarios. The key change on Solana isn't the complete replacement of Pump.fun, but rather the broadening of consumer income structures. Pump.fun proved that meme token issuance can be a high-income consumer application on Solana; Collector Crypt, on the other hand, demonstrates that tokenized physical collectibles can also generate real income, real transactions, and real user behavior.

data.title: Stripe's Ambition and the Future of Stablecoins: A Look at OUSD
data.description:
Author: EudemoniaCC Source: X, @EudemoniaCC

The stablecoin war: scenarios determine everything, channels are king, and networks win. As stablecoins begin to shift from asset competition to network competition, Stripe is also attempting to redefine the global Money Movement OS. OUSD is an inevitable convergence of stablecoin development and Stripe's strategic evolution, but it is only the beginning. Ambition awaits realization, the future has not yet arrived, and the road ahead is long and arduous. May we have a long and successful journey.

On June 30, 2026, an independent company called @openstandard launched Open USD (OUSD), with over 140 initial partners, including some who are bitter rivals.

Visa, Mastercard, American Express and other payment networks formed an alliance on OUSD. Coinbase, which enjoys at least 50%+ of the USDC reserve revenue, bet on both ends. Stripe, a payment platform that supports 1.6% of the global GDP, directly announced that OUSD would become the default stablecoin for its platform companies. In addition, the partners include financial institutions such as banks that span multiple regions in Europe, America, Asia and Africa, as well as Internet platforms such as Google, Shopify and DoorDash. As a result, the release of OUSD was also interpreted as a blatant attack on Circle by traditional financial and Internet giants. Circle's stock price fell by 17.5% on the same day. [1] It is worth mentioning that as early as June 3, Coindesk reported that @stripe planned to form an alliance to issue a new stablecoin to challenge the dominance of Circle and Tether. The CEO of Open Standard is also @zcabrams, the founder and CEO of @Stablecoin. Given that Bridge was acquired by Stripe at a valuation of 1.1 billion US dollars in 2025, Stripe is widely regarded as the core promoter of OUSD. [2] So what innovations does OUSD have that allow Stripe to raise a brand new banner for stablecoins, and why does the industry need such a new type of stablecoin and why only Stripe can and will do it? I. OUSD: Stablecoins are Moving from Asset Competition to Network Competition In fact, from the perspectives of pegging, minting, and redemption, OUSD as an asset is not particularly innovative. It remains pegged 1:1 to the US dollar, and partners can mint and redeem without limit at zero cost. The innovations pointed to by the other two design principles lie in the governance layer: Open Organization: The controlling board of directors is composed of partners for joint governance and is not subordinate to any single entity, including Stripe. Profit Sharing: It overturns the traditional stablecoin profit model where the issuer monopolizes reserve interest, returning reserve profits, after deducting a minimal management fee, to the corporate partners responsible for distribution and adoption. This chaotic organizational structure is reminiscent of Visa and Bank of America half a century ago. America decided to open up the right to issue its own credit cards and form a shared payment network for the banking industry by authorizing other banks across regions. Visa was born and became the industry standard for cards. [3] Now, Visa's joining the OUSD stablecoin payment network is like going back to the old ways. What it agrees with is industry co-construction, open standards, and network governance. It is a network that competitors jointly own and maintain. It is the solution that the financial industry has been going around for half a century. In essence, OUSD is a network after the decentralization of the right to mint coins. At present, when the differences between assets have been leveled, the key to victory has shifted to the platform network of the applied assets, which means that stablecoins have begun to move from asset competition to network competition. II. Scenarios determine everything. The three paradigm evolutions of stablecoins To understand why stablecoins have moved from asset competition to network competition, we need to understand the changes in industry demand behind the three paradigm evolutions of stablecoins. Stablecoins are essentially cross-border digital dollars, and each large-scale growth in stablecoins stems from new industry demands, corresponding to further expansion of network boundaries.

First Paradigm · Digital Dollar: Crypto Transactions Need Trustworthy On-Chain Dollars

The birth of stablecoins was due to Tether's grasp of the need for a stable value anchor for 24/7 trading in the crypto market in 2014. Cryptocurrency transaction settlement and real-time transfer of stable value require on-chain digital dollars; USDT was born, moving the dollar onto the blockchain.

... As the crypto market expands and institutions enter the market, higher requirements are placed on the credibility of assets, requiring more compliant and transparent on-chain dollars. The emergence of Circle's USDC has begun to push stablecoins further towards compliance and institutionalization. Currently, the issuance scale of USDT is about US$186 billion, and the issuance scale of USDC is about US$74 billion, which closely follows, accounting for more than 80% of the stablecoin market share. [4] Under the paradigm of digital dollars, stablecoin assets are all backed by US dollars and US Treasury yields. The difference is whether the reserves are sufficient, whether they are transparent, credible and auditable, and whether there is enough asset liquidity to support crypto market transactions. The asset logic and the transaction logic are inseparable. Stablecoins stay within the crypto world to seize the pricing power of CEX and DeFi. Liquidity is king. Second Paradigm · Platform Dollar: Platforms with their own dollars are in a predicament Stablecoins have begun to move out of the crypto industry, focusing on the financial essence of Crypto and entering the financial back-end of real commercial platforms. In 2023, PayPal launched PYUSD through the regulated Paxos platform, embedding it into its existing scenarios and user ecosystem. This supported payments for YouTube creators, merchant settlements, B2B transfers, and internal treasury, undoubtedly opening new avenues and scenarios for stablecoins. It also met PayPal's need for its own USD, offering asset segregation from other platforms, flexible deployment, and cost-effective transfers. However, this moat also became a boundary. The platform's distribution capabilities created its USD, but the platform's closed ecosystem and inter-platform competition ultimately harmed it. With PayPal's revenue growth slowing significantly—only 4.3% in 2025—PayPal, facing a mid-life crisis, has trapped PYUSD in a slowly growing closed ecosystem. Currently, PYUSD's market capitalization is approximately $2.74 billion. [5] The Third Paradigm: Network Dollars – Cross-Border Settlement Dollars Shared by Platform Networks Crypto Winter, but Stablecoin Summer. Crypto payments, as a new scenario for stablecoins outside of transactions, are gradually being validated. The crypto industry is further returning to its financial essence. Circle, as a compliant stablecoin issuer, naturally hopes to enter the payment scenario. It launched Circle Payments Network (CPN) to focus on the clearing network and issued its own public chain Arc. This is equivalent to the issuer going all out to try to control the entire process. However, Circle does not actually control any distribution channels or specific scenarios. The launch of OUSD was met with widespread support. Platforms that control users, channels, and scenarios declared their bargaining power. Whether to become a subsidized downstream or a node in their own network, internet giants and traditional financial institutions will vote with the channel traffic of specific payment scenarios. "Issuance itself does not bring any economic value beyond 'your inherent distribution capabilities'."[6] I think Catalini's statement is also the philosophy behind Stripe's choice to issue OUSD using Open Standard, designing stablecoins as shared assets rather than platform-exclusive assets, with the platform sharing the right to mint, govern, generate revenue, and build the network. This is because the scenario determines everything, the channel is king, and the network wins. If USDT and USDC completed the digitization of the US dollar, and PYUSD completed the platformization of the US dollar, then OUSD is trying to complete the platform networking of the digital dollar. In essence, this is the process by which the digital dollar begins to reorganize itself according to the network effect of the Internet after the continuous decentralization of the right to mint.

III. OUSD is an inevitable intersection of stablecoin development and Stripe's strategic evolution

After more than ten years of development, stablecoins have finally moved from the crypto world into the broader financial world. From transactions to payments, the focus of competition has also shifted from assets to networks, and the boundaries of the Stablecoin Movement are becoming wider and wider. In another independent evolutionary path, from 2010 to the present, Stripe, which started with payments, has also gone through sixteen years. From payments to collections, its capabilities have expanded to serve the entire process of corporate cash flow, and it is currently one of the largest unlisted unicorns. In 2025, the total transaction volume of enterprises on the Stripe platform will reach US$1.9 trillion, an increase of 34% from 2024, accounting for about 1.6% of global GDP. [7] When the world needs a sovereign and open cross-border payment dollar network, Stripe also hopes to transform into the infrastructure of the world's money movement. OUSD is the almost inevitable intersection of these two strategic evolutionary paths. Previously, we outlined the development of stablecoins. Next, we'll examine Stripe's development to understand its ambitions, which will help you understand why a new type of stablecoin like OUSD is most likely to be successfully developed by Stripe. To answer this question, we can divide Stripe's development into three stages. This division is not an official Stripe definition, but rather a framework I use to understand its strategic evolution.

Phase 1 · Payment OS: Internet-Native Payment Collaboration

In 2010, the Collison brothers from Ireland, @patrickc @collision, discovered a new business opportunity amidst the surging Silicon Valley startup boom—how to enable startups to quickly receive money from users. The pain point of difficulty in receiving payments became the starting point for Stripe. The brothers encapsulated complex financial infrastructure into APIs that developers could directly call. Those seven lines of code marked the internet-native transformation of payments. For the first time, payments became an application module that enterprises could call, rather than a highly complex, essential engineering project. To make it easier for any internet company to accept funds from around the world, Stripe, in addition to expanding its basic payment capabilities such as receiving and making payments, began to add global payment methods and acquired companies like Paystack, an African payment company serving 60,000 businesses, to achieve geographical expansion, focusing on horizontal development in the global market. In this stage, Stripe, as Payment OS, is positioned as the entry point for payments. The second stage, Finance OS: Internet finance deeply integrated into the customer's back-end. Payment is an industry that perfectly embodies the principle of "helping others succeed as you would like to succeed yourself." Stripe emphasizes "user first" and "our success is our success," because Stripe's rapid development is built on the rapid growth of key clients with deep integration, such as Shopify. After further upgrading the Connect product in 2015, Stripe focused on platform customers as its development center, and platform customers now account for more than 50% of their revenue. In order to better serve platform customers, Stripe began to gradually penetrate into the financial back office of enterprises in its product line. In 2021, through the acquisition of Recko, Stripe was able to realize payment reconciliation and financial data matching; in the same year, it acquired TaxJar to supplement its tax processing capabilities. These capabilities, combined with products such as Billing, Issue, and Treasury, transformed Stripe from a payment tool into an income and financial management system, thereby achieving a natural and deep binding between Stripe's growth and the growth of its customers, which is difficult to replace. Moreover, after A/B testing, customers using Stripe can achieve an increase of about 10.5% in payment conversion rate and 12% in revenue. [8] In this stage, Stripe, as Finance OS, entered the back office of enterprises from the transaction entry point. Phase Three: Money Movement OS: Globally-Born Stablecoins Empowering the AI ​​Financial Layer. In 2024, Stripe processed $1.4 trillion in total payments, a 38% year-over-year increase, achieving profitability. Following this, Stripe's strategic boundaries shifted again. Beyond its mature payment and financial capabilities, it began systematically building capabilities for holding, issuing, converting, routing, settling, and spending funds. Stablecoins are internally considered a top strategic priority, and AI is a highly focused emerging industry. OpenAI and Anthropic are both major Stripe clients. Stripe has been making continuous acquisitions in the stablecoin sector. In February 2025, it acquired stablecoin infrastructure company Bridge for $1.1 billion at a premium of approximately 5 times, expanding its stablecoin capabilities to support over 150 countries and regions from 50+, and solving asset issuance and cross-border fund routing issues. That same year, it acquired programmable wallet company Privy, valued at $230 million, to address wallet and account issues. It also partnered with Paradigm to incubate Tempo, a blockchain for payment scenarios, to solve on-chain settlement. Beyond stablecoin acquisitions in 2025, Stripe also acquired Orum, a real-time payment and multi-track routing orchestration layer for banks. In 2026, Stripe completed the acquisition of usage-based billing company Metronome, further completing its end-to-end capabilities from billing to collection to settlement to fund transfer. Essentially, Stripe is building a real-time money movement OS for the AI ​​and stablecoin era, enabling any software, especially AI agents, to automatically complete billing, payment, routing, and cross-track clearing as if calling an API. Through stablecoins, the clearing and settlement layer becomes programmable, low-cost, high-throughput, and globally accessible. Currently valued at $159 billion but delaying its IPO, Stripe resembles a patient hunter, armed with stablecoins—a weapon inherently global and always ready for action—aiming at the flow of funds in the entire internet economy and even the future AI agent economy. Stripe has formed a complete stablecoin infrastructure through Bridge, Privy, and Tempo, enabling it to own its own stablecoin. Through its B2B model, Stripe serves 5 million businesses, including all top AI companies, numerous large blue-chip companies comprising 90% of the Dow Jones Industrial Average, most leading tech companies representing 80% of the Nasdaq 100, and a large number of newly established startups (currently, 25% of new Delaware companies are created through Stripe Atlas). This is a real distribution channel and business network. Stripe has the need and the motivation to have its own stablecoin, but it also has the confidence to share the proceeds from stablecoin issuance. Card organizations, banks, internet giants, and crypto companies—these were among the first partners of OUSD and are already Stripe's partners. By sharing the proceeds from stablecoin issuance and reserves with them, Stripe can still earn revenue throughout the entire lifecycle of its funds. Issuing a closed stablecoin, on the other hand, would only undermine the neutrality of its payment network and reduce platform adoption by businesses. After all, Stripe isn't vying for the profit pool of a single stablecoin, but rather for the control plane of future global capital flows. It has the capability and the incentive to form an open stablecoin network. There's no better company than Stripe, possessing all these elements simultaneously. OUSD couldn't find a better environment elsewhere, and Stripe needed this open, rather than monopolistic, new type of stablecoin—it was inevitable. IV. Ambition Awaits Realization, the Future Hasn't Arrived Yet "We haven't won yet" is a frequently mentioned slogan within Stripe. Payments are just the starting point for Stripe; it's constantly expanding the boundaries of Money Movement. As the internet enters the AI ​​Agent era, the object of Money Movement is shifting from "people" to "machines," and new territories are yet to be reached. Regarding Agentic Payment, the payment industry is very optimistic about it, and both large and small companies are getting involved. However, in the current situation where Agent Ecommerce has not yet really taken off, the necessity and depth of stablecoin application in it are still a matter of debate. It is understood that Anthropic is currently focusing on the natural growth brought by its model capabilities and has not adopted the stablecoin payment solution recommended by Stripe. The monthly traffic of Machine Payments Protocol (MPP) jointly released by Stripe and Tempo has not exceeded $10,000 recently after the initial wave receded. [9] However, no matter where Agentic Payment develops, Stripe has already taken the lead with its surfing gear. Regarding OUSD, which appeared as a challenger alliance, it was declared to have won the first round of attacks by the capital market, but it has not yet been officially issued, which gives the feeling of "subduing the enemy without fighting". Industry practitioners are more focused on and anticipating how much deep support OUSD, launching "later this year," will receive from its listed partners. Currently, only Stripe has adopted the term "default," making a concrete commitment to driving business traffic. Visa, from 1958 to 2007, went through a process of decentralizing governance and then relisting. Is this the path OUSD wants to take? As a chaotic organization, Visa's birth was inseparable from Dee Hock, the person in charge. Will Zach remain the leader of OUSD? Visa later faced challenges from Mastercard and monopoly allegations; will the story of the dragon slayer becoming the dragon itself repeat itself? From Tether's USDT to Circle's USDC, and then to PayPal's PYUSD and other new stablecoins, none of the challengers have completely overturned, replaced, or even surpassed the pioneers; they have only weakened the market expectations and imaginations of the former. Will Open Standard's OUSD be an exception because it opens up the traditional financial payment market? All questions end with a question mark, because Stripe's ambition to enter the AI ​​Agent era and its Money Movement ambitions are still waiting to be realized. After three paradigm shifts, the future of network competition for stablecoins has not yet truly arrived. The road ahead is long and arduous, but may we have a long and successful journey.

@EudemoniaCC

A Crypto Payment professional who loves cooking, rock climbing, and writing, recently resigned and traveled to Italy, New York, and Silicon Valley. After a rock climbing accident, he urgently returned to China and is currently in seclusion at home. He is currently unemployed. Previously, while serving as Head of APAC at @MorphNetwork, a public blockchain focused on payments under @bitget, he was a communications enthusiast who created a phenomenal Card Campaign with over a million views with zero budget (actually, he studied this; he just graduated from Tsinghua University last year. If he hadn't chosen Crypto in the summer of 2022, he might be a tech journalist now).

data.title: How Michael Saylor Quietly Replaced Bitcoin With Credit
data.description:
For years, Michael Saylor built his reputation as Bitcoin's most outspoken evangelist, relentlessly promoting BTC while criticizing the debt-driven financial system and fiat credit.

But beginning in mid-2025, that messaging underwent a striking transformation and Saylor started shifted his focus away from Bitcoin itself and toward dollar-denominated credit products, a pivot that ultimately culminated in the launch of Strategy's STRC security.

The FUD: “Saylor will sell Bitcoin.”

The reality:

If selling 1 BTC protects credit investors, supports the equity, and allows Strategy to raise capital to buy 20 more BTC, that is economically rational. https://t.co/W75pG8nJGLpic.twitter.com/e84czAJrdf

— Alex 👽 (@AlexesNakamoto) July 1, 2026

The shift marks one of the biggest changes in Saylor's public narrative since Strategy first embraced Bitcoin in 2020.

From attacking credit to selling it

Between August 2020 and June 2025, Saylor published nearly 3,500 posts on X, with roughly 76% referencing Bitcoin. Mentions of credit were almost nonexistent and typically framed as criticism of the traditional financial system that Bitcoin was designed to replace.

That changed dramatically after June 2025.

Rather than portraying credit as Bitcoin's enemy, Saylor began introducing an entirely new vocabulary around Strategy's capital structure. Bitcoin became "digital capital," MSTR shares were described as "digital equity," and the newly launched STRC was marketed as "digital credit."

Strategy's stock is having its second big pop this week$MSTR jumped as much as 13% Wednesday, extending a rebound that started Monday when Michael Saylor unveiled a new Digital Credit Capital Framework for the company.

The framework: a combined $2B in buyback authority split… pic.twitter.com/aoh5cBe8ZB

— BSCN (@BSCNews) July 1, 2026

The messaging extended beyond social media. Strategy increasingly emphasized dollar-denominated products, promoted dividend-paying securities and continued issuing USD-based instruments designed to finance additional Bitcoin purchases.

STRC was supposed to trade like credit

STRC was designed to occupy a different role within Strategy's growing collection of securities. The company promoted the product as a stable, income-generating instrument targeting a $100 par value while paying dividends in US dollars.

For several months, the strategy appeared to work. Between late 2025 and much of the first half of 2026, STRC largely traded around its intended value. That stability broke down this summer.

The security slid as low as $71.25, nearly 30% below its intended price, while MSTR also continued its prolonged decline. Last week, Strategy's flagship stock traded near $82, a sharp drop from its 52-week high and another sign that investor confidence in the company's increasingly complex capital structure had weakened.

Despite being marketed as "digital credit," STRC differs significantly from traditional fixed-income products. Unlike a corporate bond, it carries no requirement for Strategy to hold assets backing the security, offers no redemption right at its stated par value and is not collateralized by the company's Bitcoin holdings.

Investors also receive none of the protections associated with traditional banking or brokerage products, making STRC fundamentally an equity-based instrument despite its credit-oriented branding.

At the same time, Saylor's description of Bitcoin also evolved. Rather than referring to BTC as digital money, he increasingly characterized it as a long-term capital asset capable of compounding at roughly 30% annually, despite Bitcoin's five-year annualized growth rate being materially lower.

The strategy faces its biggest test

Recent market performance has placed Strategy's model under growing pressure.

Bitcoin has fallen more than 50% from its peak above $126,000, while Strategy's common shares have lost roughly 78% over the past year. Earlier this month, the company's enterprise value briefly fell below the value of its Bitcoin holdings for the first time.

🟢 Bitcoin Price: $61,000 pic.twitter.com/adoZzTRWH5

— Bitcoin Price (@BTCPrice) July 2, 2026

Strategy also completed its first voluntary Bitcoin sale since December 2022, reversing years of public messaging that the company had no intention of selling its BTC.

Even as Saylor continues to publicly champion Bitcoin, the company's strategy increasingly revolves around credit products and capital engineering rather than the digital asset alone.

For critics, the transformation is difficult to ignore: the executive who once argued Bitcoin would replace the credit system now appears to be betting that credit is the vehicle that will keep his Bitcoin strategy alive.

data.title: A comprehensive look at Robinhood's ambitions behind its AI In-chain finance.
data.description:
Tao Zhu, Jinse Finance

Summary:On July 2, 2026, Robinhood Chain officially launched its public mainnet. This L2 platform is built on Arbitrum technology, with Uniswap and others as its first partners. Stock Tokens were launched simultaneously, supporting over 120 countries. Qualified users can trade 24/7 through Robinhood Wallet. With the Robinhood mainnet, stock tokens, AI trading, and DeFi all in place, the era of on-chain finance has arrived.

I. Robinhood's Five Global Expansion Strategies

Today, Robinhood Markets held a keynote speech in London titled "Robinhood Presents: The World is Flat," announcing Robinhood's global expansion plans and product vision. Robinhood is breaking down traditional financial barriers, allowing customers to access global markets in ways never before possible.

Robinhood Chain Officially Launched

The Robinhood Chain mainnet is built on the Arbitrum platform, meets institutional-grade standards, and is natively connected to Robinhood's on-chain users. This Layer 2 blockchain provides developers with an out-of-the-box environment.

Initial partners include Uniswap and Pleiades. Uniswap is deploying a dedicated AMM as its primary public liquidity protocol, while Pleiades is deploying a proprietary AMM as its primary proprietary trading platform. Robinhood Chain is deeply integrated with industry-leading platforms such as Alchemy, BitGo, and Chainlink, boasting fast block generation speeds and out-of-the-box DeFi primitives, such as lending. Robinhood Chain natively supports AI technology and is built for real-world assets, providing developers with a permissionless environment for seamless innovation. Stock Token Currently, the Robinhood wallet has launched a stock token in over 120 countries, but specific availability varies by jurisdiction. Eligible users can unlock numerous features with the new stock token, such as 24/7 trading on the Robinhood chain and increasing asset returns through activities such as deploying the token to lending pools and using it as collateral for trading in the broader DeFi ecosystem. The stock tokens are tokenized debt securities issued by Robinhood Assets (Jersey) Limited, providing economic exposure to the underlying securities but not granting investors any legal rights or beneficial interests in those securities, nor any rights against the issuers of those securities. Eligible users can trade them spot through the Robinhood wallet via decentralized exchanges such as Uniswap, Rialto, Lighter, Arcus, and 1Inch. Robinhood Earn is rolling out Robinhood Earn to eligible US users, the first decentralized lending product directly accessible through the Robinhood main app. Robinhood Earn allows users to borrow USDG backed by USD through a self-custodied wallet, with an expected annualized yield of 7%. The product is insured by Lloyd's of London and RELM to protect users from losses in the event of cybersecurity or smart contract attacks. Its underlying lending infrastructure is based on the Morpho protocol and is supported by industry-leading partners such as Steakhouse, Ethena, Spark, and Maple. This means Robinhood is no longer just a trading platform, but is beginning to bring users into the DeFi ecosystem. Agentic Trading For a long time, large institutions have used high-frequency algorithms to capture market opportunities, while retail traders have often been at a disadvantage. To bridge this gap, Robinhood is preparing to launch a smart agent account for cryptocurrencies. With Robinhood's "Trading MCP," eligible US traders can connect their chosen AI model to Robinhood's data sources and tools. The user's smart agent can continuously scan massive amounts of data points and execute trading strategies the instant market conditions change. The smart agent trading feature for cryptocurrencies will soon be available to eligible US traders at no additional cost.

International Expansion

Robinhood already serves nearly 28 million customers across 38 countries and three continents. In accelerating its international expansion, Robinhood plans to launch its crypto business in Canada, introduce crypto trading in the UK, expand its perpetual contract products in Europe, and obtain a capital markets services license in Singapore to advance its local operations. Robinhood's goal is no longer limited to the US market, but to build a global financial platform.

II. Understanding Robinhood's Ambition

"The World Is Flat" is not just a new product launch, but a declaration of Robinhood's ambition to become the future financial infrastructure. Robinhood is creating a legend of leaping from a trading platform to on-chain financial infrastructure.

Johann Kerbrat, Senior Vice President and General Manager of Cryptocurrency and International Business at Robinhood, stated, “DeFi unlocks possibilities that traditional finance couldn’t provide, but historically, navigating DeFi has required specialized technical expertise. We are combining the strengths of traditional finance and DeFi to extend financial ownership to every corner of the globe.” Robinhood’s initiative aims to establish itself as a super financial app for the on-chain era: tokenizing traditional financial assets, hosting these on-chain assets on its own public blockchain, improving user trading efficiency through AI Agents, and introducing DeFi to allow users to earn on-chain rewards while trading. Specifically, firstly, more and more trading platforms are building their own blockchain infrastructure, and now Robinhood has joined this movement, unifying trading, wallets, payments, asset issuance, and other businesses on-chain. In the future, Robinhood may control the entire value chain, not just be a trading gateway. Secondly, Robinhood tightly integrates stocks with the DeFi world. As Robinhood states, eligible users will not only be able to trade stock tokens 24/7, but also deploy them to lending protocols to earn yields, or use them as collateral to participate in more DeFi applications. This eliminates trading time restrictions, cross-border investment processes, and shortens liquidation cycles for stocks, ultimately leading to their integration with DeFi. Stocks may only be the first step; in the future, more traditional financial assets may be tokenized, with stocks, bonds, and funds becoming foundational assets in the DeFi world. Thirdly, Robinhood is attempting to transform trading with AI. Robinhood's Agentic Trading allows users to connect large language models to Robinhood's data interfaces and trading tools, enabling ordinary traders to automate trades once AI identifies opportunities. Finally, a global vision is further expanding Robinhood's business scope. Business plans in Canada, the UK, Europe, and Singapore will give wings to Robinhood's ambitions.

III. Why is Robinhood Betting on On-Chain Finance Now?

Improved Regulatory Environment

Whether it's the improvement of the stablecoin regulatory framework or the public deployment of RWA by traditional financial institutions, both are sending a clear signal to the industry—the crypto industry is having a more certain future. For Robinhood, the increasingly clear regulatory environment means that it can more boldly promote innovative businesses such as stock tokenization.

The RWA Sector is Hot

RWA is becoming a new consensus among global financial institutions. Whether it's BlackRock's tokenized fund BUIDL, Franklin Templeton's on-chain money fund, or JPMorgan Chase's Tokenized Collateral Network, more and more traditional financial institutions are starting to try the RWA sector. Robinhood can directly introduce traditional assets such as stocks and ETFs into the on-chain ecosystem, becoming a bridge connecting the crypto and real worlds.

Brokerages Seek Transformation
Just weeks ago, Robinhood announced it would lay off approximately 10% of its workforce, with restructuring costs expected to reach $28 million, a move aimed at transitioning to a leaner, more "efficient" operating model. Meanwhile, Robinhood's cryptocurrency business performed poorly in the first quarter, with cryptocurrency revenue falling 47% year-over-year to $134 million and native application cryptocurrency trading volume declining 48% to $24 billion.

Robinhood's new plan may reverse its current predicament.

Summary

The lines between cryptocurrency and traditional finance are becoming increasingly blurred. Robinhood is steadily expanding, moving beyond stock and spot crypto trading to establish itself as an on-chain financial infrastructure.

As Forbes stated: Robinhood no longer positions itself as a brokerage firm for young investors. It aspires to be a gateway to a faster, more global, and more speculative financial market where stocks, tokens, derivatives, prediction markets, and AI agents are increasingly integrated into a single application.

data.title: A16z's latest move aims to set a "price" for computing power.
data.description: The story of AI has been told for a long time, with different protagonists emerging one after another. First, there were large models, then AI agents, then data centers, electricity, and so on. With each wave of hot money flowing in, capital is looking for that area "not yet priced in." Now, it's time to talk about computing power itself. On June 24th, Ornn, a company founded just one year ago, announced the completion of a $33 million seed round, led by a16z Crypto, with participation from Galaxy Ventures, Nordstar, and SV Angel. Existing investors Vine Ventures, Crucible Capital, Link Ventures, and Box Group also invested. A seed round of $33 million doesn't seem particularly impressive on its own. What's more intriguing is why a16z invested. The answer lies in what Ornn wants to do. It doesn't intend to sell computing power; it wants to set a "price" for it. The ultimate limit of computing power is, of course, electricity. But before reaching that end, it may first need its own "oil price." Computing power doesn't yet have its own "oil price." To understand Ornn's positioning, we need to look at today's computing power market. The answer is straightforward: it's a huge spot market. Today, buying and selling GPU computing power is basically still on a "one-off negotiation" basis. The buyer and seller negotiate a price privately, and the price changes again for the next transaction with a different counterparty. There's no universally accepted benchmark for "what's the current price" in the market; forward curves and hedging tools are relatively distant. What does this mean? It's like the whole world is burning oil, but there's no WTI or Brent crude; the price of each barrel depends entirely on on-the-spot bargaining. Looking at the key information from this funding round together reveals that this isn't just another AI project. These two MIT graduates aren't building a GPU cloud; they're laying the foundation for a financial market. This product framework comprises three parts: a price index OCPI, a derivative product entry point, and Ornn Compute, a platform that revitalizes the capacity of physical GPUs. Ornn's three products precisely address these gaps. The first part, OCPI, is the "oil price" of computing power. It's a computing power index calculated from actual transactions, not just listed prices, covering mainstream models like the H100, A100, H200, B200, and RTX 5090. Reportedly, the index has been running for six months, is listed on Bloomberg Terminal, and is used by over 400 operators, investors, and AI companies. ICE's announcement further confirms Ornn's direction. ICE stated it will launch a series of GPU compute futures based on OCPI with Ornn, with contracts denominated in USD and settled in cash; The second aspect is futures and options, providing hedging tools for computing power. Back in May, Ornn and ICE announced a partnership to launch USD-denominated, cash-settled GPU computing power futures, using OCPI as the reference rate. AI companies can go long, locking in future training costs; data centers can go short, locking in future revenue. The third component, Ornn Compute, transforms idle capacity into liquid assets. It aggregates dedicated GPU capacity from multiple Neocloud providers onto a single platform, unifying access and supporting secondary transfers and on-demand subleasing. Operators can connect with diverse tenants with a single offtake agreement, and buyers can clearly see the cluster's sites, hardware, and terms. Capacity that was originally locked in long-term contracts and had very low liquidity has been transformed into something akin to "listable warehouse receipts." This is more like a "computing power version" of ICE/Platts, aiming to earn "tolls" from the entire market. The core pain point it aims to address is clear: currently, computing power contracts are almost entirely negotiated one-on-one between buyers and sellers, resulting in highly dispersed prices. Operators have virtually no visibility into the utilization rate and resale value after the 3-5 year contract expires, yet they bear the duration risk alone. This is a market with a large spot market but almost entirely lacking in financial layer. Ornn's chain logic is also a restatement of a16z's logic: Reliable pricing data → Price discovery → Risk transfer → More efficient capacity allocation. Looking back at a16z's choice, the logic becomes much clearer. A16z's previous investment views were very straightforward: we are in the midst of "the biggest capital formation event of our lifetime." Over the next decade, trillions of dollars will flood into GPUs, data centers, and electricity, making this construction one of the largest industrial mobilizations in human history. The money is already on its way, but the market structure to absorb it hasn't been established yet. Mature commodity markets typically develop in a relatively fixed order: reliable price data precedes price discovery; price discovery allows for risk transfer; and risk transfer enables efficient capacity allocation. Each of Ornn's products is stuck at a crucial point in this chain. If we break it down according to the structure of financial markets, Ornn's goals can be roughly divided into four levels: Ornn hopes to become the benchmark price layer, risk transfer layer, and capacity trading layer of compute. This is the mismatch that a16z saw, and the fundamental reason why it bet early. First, the spot market is already massive, but the financial layer is almost nonexistent. a16z isn't investing in a cloud service, but rather in equipping this emerging commodity with the underlying pipeline for "price discovery + risk transfer." This is a classic case of "the shovel seller among shovel sellers": while others are scrambling to sell GPUs, it wants to sell the rules that allow GPUs to be priced and traded. Second, the cooperation with ICE is a key signal of "validate first, invest later." The ICE futures partnership took place in May, even before the seed round announcement. This means that when a16z placed its bet, Ornn had likely already secured the endorsement and clearing channels of a top global commodity exchange. In other words, the product's "settibility" had already been accepted by the traditional financial system, and a large portion of the bet's risk had been mitigated in advance. What a16z was waiting for was likely this point in time when the "traditional financial interface was working." Third, securing a position as a "standard setter." There's an ironclad rule in the commodity market: the benchmark price is often winner-takes-all. Crude oil is judged by WTI and Brent, interest rates by LIBOR and SOFR, and there's usually only one dominant benchmark. Whoever's index is adopted by the exchange as a clearing reference controls the entire industry's revenue stream. a16z isn't looking for an ordinary player, but rather a "benchmark price monopolist" in the computing power era. And such a window of opportunity often only exists in the very early stages of an industry. The real intention behind this is... AI computing power is ready for commercialization: huge demand, fragmented supply, opaque pricing, and unhedgeable balance sheet risks. Ornn's ultimate goal is to transform GPU-hours into underlying financial assets like crude oil, electricity, interest rates, or mortgages. This is a16z's bet on "transforming computing power from a cloud service resource into a priced, tradable, and financeable commodity." This strategy is increasingly ambitious. The first layer is seizing the pricing power of computing power. If OCPI truly becomes the WTI of the computing power world, then all future contracts, financings, and derivatives referencing it will inevitably involve Ornn. This business is more sophisticated than operating a GPU cloud. Clouds profit from capacity price differences, while indices and settlement benchmarks profit from the "infrastructure tax" of the entire market. The second layer is making AI computing power a completely new asset class. With benchmark prices and derivatives, dedicated GPU capacity transforms from a "heavy asset burden" into a "liquid asset": idle capacity can be activated, and long-term risks can be hedged. A whole ecosystem surrounding it, including lending, structured transactions, and market making, may emerge. a16z aspires to be a "founding shareholder" of this new asset class. The third layer involves establishing a brand for the fund itself. Investing a crypto fund in a computing power market operating on the tracks of traditional exchanges like ICE is itself a declaration: the financial engineering capabilities accumulated from Web3 are spilling over into the much larger physical market of AI. Ornn is cutting into the next lower layer, which is the financial market infrastructure: pricing, clearing, hedging, secondary liquidity of capacity, and collateral valuation. The financial primitives that Web3 excels at—pricing, liquidity, and risk transfer—have been moved from digital assets to the physical computing power of AI. Following this line of thought, the on-chain perpetual nature of computing power indices, the RWA-based transformation of GPU capacity, the stablecoin settlement of NeoCloud's cash flow, and the DePIN network's direct use of OCPI pricing—these new forms are beginning to offer room for imagination. In other words, the core production factors of AI are beginning to be recoded by the capital market. How to find the anchor point? If we were to find a historical benchmark, the most similar investment to this one might not be any particular AI infrastructure funding round, but rather the Series A round in 2020 led by a16z for Uniswap. In August 2020, a16z led an $11 million investment. At the time, the market was still debating whether AMMs and DEXs were niche toys, but capital had already sensed the signal: on-chain trading would grow into an independent financial market structure. The rest is history; Uniswap became a crucial part of the entire DeFi trading layer. You can also refer to a16z's $23 million investment in OpenSea in 2021. OpenSea targets the NFT trading market as the entry point into this cycle; what they're really doing is redefining "how the market itself is formed." The common thread lies here: when a sector is just emerging and infrastructure is still lacking, they bet on the "rule-maker." A16z led the investment, not betting on the GPU cloud business, but on a more fundamental trend: AI infrastructure will eventually develop its own price benchmarks, derivatives markets, and capital allocation systems, just like energy, interest rates, mortgages, and crypto assets.

data.title: The demand for intelligence is almost limitless, but the cost is exorbitant.
data.description:
Article Author: Bryan DaughertyArticle Compilation：Block unicorn

For a long time, since OpenAI made large-scale language models available to millions of households, artificial intelligence was like a new toy that could be played with at will. It wasn't until the new models began to consume large amounts of energy and resources that many people realized the reality that artificial intelligence had reached its performance limits.

Tokenmaxxing: Optimize for Your Own Clients

This is somewhat self-inflicted.

The common practice across the industry is to use the largest model and the deepest inference setup for every task.

The common practice across the industry is to use the largest model and the deepest inference setup for every task.

If you leave this decision to the users, you'll get this—they'll handle everything with Opus 4.8/GPT-5.5 because, why not? But this behavior silently kills ROI. The latest inference models make token burning so easy. They generate lengthy internal chains of logic before providing an answer, and you have to pay for every hidden token. A request that returns a few hundred visible words can actually consume thousands of billed tokens, meaning the real cost of each useful answer can be an order of magnitude higher than the cost shown on the sticker. In a comparative evaluation, the top-performing inference model cost approximately 14 times more than a lower-cost general-purpose model when performing the same task, and failed four times as many times. Indiscriminately applying the highest level of intelligence actually leads to higher costs and worse performance. A deeper problem is the mismatch in incentives—sellers are paid in tokens, while buyers are paid based on the transaction outcome. The token maximization strategy maximizes the first objective but weakens the second. It pushes the price of each decision above the demand curve, far from the actual bottom of demand, which is precisely when the system needs to pull prices down to the bottom. You can't satisfy near-infinite demand by selling the most expensive product to everyone. You should price a sufficiently good solution within your return on investment and let sales meet demand. Why "costs will always decrease" doesn't solve the problem. I know you're about to bring up the "Jeves paradox," but that won't get you out of there. First, the cost that needs to be reduced is the cost of each valid result, not the cost of each token. There may be a correlation between the two, but only if we address the aforementioned behavioral issues. If the token price falls, and token maximization accelerates the increase in token consumption per task, then the cost of completing the actual work will not decrease at all, and may even increase. The coexistence of falling unit prices and rising bills is a contradiction I've heard some teams are currently experiencing. Second, cost savings must truly benefit the buyer. If efficiency gains are entirely captured by sellers or wasted on unnecessary justifications, the return on investment will never be achieved, and market demand will remain unmet. If the token cost of the model is reduced, but the underlying inference and tool calls increase token consumption, it's hard to say whether the result itself will become cheaper. This is a market problem, not a procurement problem. If only enterprise software were disappointing, it wouldn't be a big deal. But today, a large portion of the global economy is staked on "AI trade," so any misstep could have a huge impact. Major U.S. hyperscale data center operators will spend over $1 trillion in 2025-2026. This spending is based on projected future revenue, which depends on enterprise adoption, which in turn depends on ROI. The gap between capital expenditures and revenue is being bridged through financing, some of which appears quite cyclical. I don't think it will be that serious yet, but we need to connect all of this to the demand curve. In its simplest terms, if demand is not infinite but nearly infinite, and is constrained by an unrealized ROI, then the biggest risk is that the revenue justifying capital expenditures will not materialize on time. Financing cycles cannot forever replace real cash flow. A technology with such high leverage and concentration, if it fails to achieve its intended effects, will inevitably trigger significant economic upheaval. Frankly, if the return on investment doesn't materialize on a large scale, AI transactions will begin to crumble, and a spontaneous, cyclical, centralized structure will disintegrate in the very way it was built: rapidly and simultaneously affecting everyone. This is the worst-case scenario. I'm not saying this is the most likely outcome, but it begins with companies becoming dissatisfied with pilot projects and starting to restrict demand. "Returns on investment take time"—yes, and that's precisely where the danger lies. The most serious objection to the issue of ROI is the timing. Any general-purpose technology has a lag, and the returns from that lag can be real. But its lag may not be as apparent as the quarterly cycles that drive the market. This mismatch manifests itself between the slow pace of organizational restructuring and the fast-paced financing structures of capital markets. This technology could bring about real change over decades, during which time trade finance may be disrupted. Both could happen simultaneously. This is why time is the scarcest resource here, and why we need to act quickly. The current state of ROI isn't entirely bleak, and that's not the conclusion I want you to draw. Some businesses are indeed seeing measurable returns now. However, in many cases, these companies are not the ones pursuing the smartest models and committing to the broadest empowerment. It refers to teams performing narrow, bounded, repeatable workloads, using models suitable for the work, and practically measuring results. Return on investment (ROI) is evident in companies that deploy intelligent routing—sending simple queries to low-cost models and only escalating complex queries. ROI is also evident in teams that replace cutting-edge models with smaller, more refined models for a large number of structured tasks. Return on investment (ROI) is evident in companies that employ prompting and context-filtering tools to reduce the amount of content sent to the most expensive, most inference-driven models. ROI is also evident in projects that build custom agent frameworks that manage tool access, memory, permissions, workflow status, evaluation, logging, human approval, rollback mechanisms, and cost control. These are not examples of weak AI capabilities. They simply produce the same results, but below the ROI threshold, rather than above it. Early achievers, those who reaped returns on their investments, all followed one principle: pursue wisdom with every dollar, not pursue wisdom at all costs. The entire premise of tokenmaxxing measures the wrong numbers. The number that determines whether the entire AI trading is sustainable is the return per dollar. Effective work delivered divided by delivery costs. This ratio determines whether a use case falls from above the ROI line to below it, and the demand waiting below the line is the only factor sufficient to justify the invested capital. Therefore, the urgent task, the truly pressing work, is to drastically reduce the cost of each outcome quickly enough to bring a large number of shelved use cases under consideration and get these approaches adopted before the market loses patience. The main leverage is already in place: assign each task to the cheapest model capable of actually performing it, instead of defaulting to the most expensive model. Simplify the context and inference, ensuring it contains only what the task requires, rather than cramming everything into a maximum model with maximum settings. Encapsulate the agent within a framework of control tools, memory, evaluation, approval, and budgeting, so reliability comes from the system, not from the size of the brute-force model. Fine-tune a smaller, open-weight model using proprietary data to handle high-volume, repeatable workloads that don't require cutting-edge technology. Each method accomplishes the same task at a lower cost, and only in this way can the near-infinite demand be transformed into tangible results. Optimistically, all of this indicates that market demand truly exists. It is enormous, poised to take off, and the key factor determining its success or failure is singular—return on investment. We simply need to work together to make this happen as quickly as possible.

data.title: ETF withdrawals and regulatory pressures combine; the market is still awaiting a trend reversal.
data.description:
Over the past two weeks, the crypto market has continued to weaken due to a combination of factors, including persistent outflows from ETFs, on-chain discounts in BTC, short-term holders being trapped, and increasing regulatory enforcement pressure. The market has not yet shifted from "passive absorption" to "active buying."

Core Judgment

Market Status:The global total market capitalization of crypto has fallen from approximately $2.27 trillion to approximately $2.05 trillion, a pullback of about 9.7%, indicating that risk appetite remains weak.

Key Pressures: The Fear & Greed Index has fallen to 17, and the spot Bitcoin ETF saw a net outflow of approximately $4 billion in June, indicating weakening institutional buying support. Funding Structure: BTC Dominance remains around 57%, and ETH around 9%, indicating that funds are still concentrated in mainstream assets and have not yet spread to high-beta assets. On-Chain Structure: BTC is trading at approximately $62,300, a discount of about 19% from the True Market Mean of approximately $77,000, still within a structural bear market range. Regulatory Direction: Changes in MiCA, Australian travel rules, and the independence of US regulatory agencies are increasing market compliance costs and policy uncertainty. Market Overview: Market data shows that crypto assets continued to be under pressure this period. The global total market capitalization fell from approximately $2.27 trillion in mid-June to approximately $2.05 trillion, a pullback of about 9.7%. BTC still accounts for about 57%, ETH about 9%, and Others about 33%, indicating that funds have not significantly spread from BTC to ETH or more volatile altcoins. The sentiment is also weak. The Fear & Greed Index has fallen to 17, entering the extreme fear zone. Compared to price declines, the significance of sentiment indicators lies in the fact that they reflect the market's combined pricing of macroeconomic pressures, ETF redemptions, and spot selling pressure. In the short term, if sentiment cannot recover from the extreme fear zone to the neutral zone, rebounds are more likely to be seen as a window for reducing positions rather than a trend reversal signal.
